4 min read

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Day 12 of #MasterDSA! Today’s problem is one of the most asked interview questions out there, and it’s a beautiful example of the sliding window flavor of two pointers we first touched on back in Day 8. Let’s break it down.
The Problem
Given a string s, find the length of the longest substring that doesn't contain any repeating characters.
📎 Try it yourself on LeetCode: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
Examples
Input: "abcabcbb"
Output: 3
Explanation: The answer is "abc", with a length of 3.Input: "bbbbb"
Output: 1
Explanation: The answer is "b" - every character after that is a repeat.Input: "pwwkew"
Output: 3
Explanation: The answer is "wke", length 3.Note: "pwke" is a subsequence (skips characters), not a substring
(which must be contiguous) - so it doesn't count!
Rules of the game

• 0 <= s.length <= 50,000

• s can contain letters, digits, symbols, and spaces
The Key Insight
Checking every possible substring for duplicates would mean looking at O(n²) substrings, and checking each one for repeats could cost even more on top of that. Way too slow.
Here’s the smarter way to think about it:
Keep a window of characters that has no duplicates. Grow it by moving the right pointer forward. The moment you hit a duplicate, shrink the window from the left side — removing characters one by one — until the duplicate is gone.
This is exactly the “growing and shrinking window” pattern from Day 8’s Subarray Product problem, just applied to characters instead of numbers.
The Two-Pointer (Sliding Window) Approach

• Keep a left pointer starting at 0, and a set called window to track which characters are currently inside our window.

• Move right across the string, one character at a time:

• While s[right] is already in window (a duplicate!), remove s[left] from the window and move left forward — keep doing this until the duplicate character is gone.

• Once there’s no duplicate left, insert s[right] into the window.

• Update maxLength with the current window size: right - left + 1.
3. By the time right reaches the end of the string, maxLength holds your answer.
Think of it like a hallway where you keep letting new guests in — but the moment someone with a name tag matching a current guest shows up, you start kicking people out from the front of the line until that name is free again.
The Code (C++)
class Solution {
public:
int lengthOfLongestSubstring(string s) {
int left = 0;
// tracks which characters are currently inside our window
unordered_set<char> window;
int maxLength = 0;
// move the right pointer to include a new character
for (int right = 0; right < s.length(); right++) {
// if we hit a duplicate, shrink the window from the left
// until that duplicate character is gone
while (window.count(s[right])) {
window.erase(s[left]);
left++;
}
// now it's safe to add the new character
window.insert(s[right]);
maxLength = max(maxLength, right - left + 1);
}
return maxLength;
}
};Dry Run
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Final answer: 3 — matching the expected "abc" (or any of its rotations found along the way).
Notice at right = 6 and right = 7, the shrinking step had to remove two characters in a row before the duplicate was fully cleared — that's the while loop doing exactly what it's meant to do.
How Fast/Efficient Is This?

• Time Complexity: O(n) — even though there’s a while loop nested inside the for loop, left only ever moves forward across the entire run, so each character is visited by left and right at most once each.

• Space Complexity: O(min(n, m)), where m is the size of the possible character set (like 128 for ASCII). The window can never hold more unique characters than exist in the alphabet you're using.
Edge Cases Worth Remembering

• Empty string: "" → 0, loop never runs.

• All identical characters: "bbbbb" → 1, the window keeps shrinking back down to size 1 every time.

• All unique characters: "abcdef" → the full length, since the window never needs to shrink.

• Repeats that aren’t adjacent: "pwwkew" → make sure the window logic correctly shrinks past the first "w" when the second one shows up.
Keep Practicing — Related Problems

• Subarray Product Less Than K

• Longest Repeating Character Replacement

• Minimum Window Substring
Takeaways from Today

• The sliding window pattern isn’t just for numbers and products — it works beautifully on strings and character sets too.

• A while loop inside your for loop doesn't always mean worse time complexity — as long as your shrinking pointer only ever moves forward overall, the whole thing stays linear.

• Using a set (or sometimes a hash map with last-seen indices) is a common companion tool for sliding window problems where you need to quickly check “is this already in my window?”
See you tomorrow for Day 13!
